comments.title: Jesus Malverde
comments.content: I recall a few weeks ago, you mentioned that Santa Muerte isn't the negative or malevolent being that some might imagine.  I've since found a decent book that has helped me to start offering devotion to her.  The book mentioned another Catholic folk saint, Jesus Malverde.  I'm very interested in him and perhaps setting up a shrine to him.  Do you happen to know much about him?  He's sometimes called the narco saint but I'm going to assume that connection means as little as it does with Santa Muerte.  I also welcome any comments from the commentariat about this folk saint, or Santa Muerte.

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: I know very little about St. Jesus Malverde, though I've seen his images and candles in botanicas and well-stocked hoodoo shops. Anyone else?

replies.content:
He was a bandit, that’s all I know.

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: The stories I've found so far make him appear to be a Mexican Robin Hood-like figure.  It also isn't certain that he's an historical person, with several Mexican bandits from the 19th century perhaps coalescing into the legends we now have.  But, then again, Holy Death isn't exactly an historical figure, either, yet she's very effective at helping her devotees.  Which leads me to a second question for JMG: if there's no historical person behind the figure of St. Jesus Malverde, is it reasonable to posit that a spirit of some manner is the being that answers the prayers of his devotees?  Really, my primary concern in starting devotion to him is whether he's a beneficent being or not.  I'd rather not work with some malevolent force, like a folk saint equivalent of demonic Jesii.

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content:
I went to sleep thinking about this question and upon waking this morning, I've got this incredible sense of peace. I'm trying not to read too much into this feeling but I don't usually wake up with a feeling of serenity like this. I don't recall a dream last night on this matter. At the very least, my subconscious seems to have worked out something during my sleep.

I think it is time to turn to divination for some assistance, since St. Jesus Malverde doesn't seem to have as many English speaking devotees online. I'm a bit hesitant with this, as divination has traditionally been problematic in Catholicism. That also means I'm a bit new to the method that I've recently taken up using, which is the Tarot. I'll try something simple, like drawing a single card, and if you don't mind, I'll post the card and my interpretation of it to this comment. I'd like to see how my interpretation strikes you and/or the commentariat. Will that be okay?

Second question: if one is uncertain about a spiritual being's nature or intentions, but wants to cautiously approach them, is there any advice on how to do so?

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: Okay, I asked if my primary Deity, the Holy Trinity, would approve of me starting a devotion to St. Jesus Malverde and I drew the Ace of Swords.  My initial impression was that this is an affirmative answer.  But I also got the impression that the card was telling me that working with folk saints will be different than working with canonical saints.  Does this interpretation seem plausible?  Or have I misunderstood?

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: That seems like a very plausible interpretation. Was the card upright -- well, if you do reversals?

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: Yes, and yes, the card was upright.

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content: Then definitely a favorable reading. I tend to read the Ace of Swords upright as saying "Yes, but you'll have to work at it -- this won't happen without effort."

replies.title: Re: Jesus Malverde
replies.content:
He seems to be based on an actual person but has since hybridized with other people into a Robin Hood like figure. He strikes me as the kind of figure who will evolve with time and cultural needs, changing until he's no longer recognizable as the historic person he may have once been. Future Mexican King Arthur maybe?
Sante Muerte is another evolving saint, most likely originating in Aztec folk memories of Mictecacihuatl, a goddess of the dead. What she'll eventually morph into is anyone's guess but she will likely retain her connections to the dead. I have included her as a minor character in the book I'm writing. I portray her as an ambiguous figure, not really pure evil or good but a mix of both.

JLfromNH/Dijon Fuzzy Jackalope

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content:
I don’t know about the best way, but you can begin by appreciating the planets in their natural setting. Watching the wandering stars, you begin to become aware of their inherent properties; The great Sun shining warming and burning, the Moon and her brisk dances and her many faces, Mercury closely hugging the Sun, flitting closer and farther away, bright Venus in twilight that lingers at sunset or rises as a herald for the sunrise, red Mars advancing and retreating in marches sometimes brightly sometimes dimly, brilliant Jupiter puttering within a sign for a year returning where it started when the baby startlingly grew up into a youth, grey Saturn lumbering in long circuits that mark the ages of man. Tonight, Venus, Moon, Saturn, Sunday are all strung along the evening sky, look up and learn how to use your hands to estimate angles in the sky.

As far as books, the old A to Z by George Llewelyn the man himself (Amazon has facsimiles of the 1920s editions) is a compilation of the various articles he wrote on the subject. The hardcover of the 1960s version is worth it for the inner covers that have a pithy summary in the charts and tables. Being a compilation, its uneven and yet full of star-lore, pity the ‘new’ A to Z did a number on the contents instead of rearranging them in a systematic manner.

Parker’s astrology is a presented like a high school textbook. As such, it is fairly complete and concise for its purpose as an introductory text. Its one failing is that Parker insists on the whole house system, and as a result it glosses over analytical possibilities that arise from other house systems like Placidus.

Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson’s oveur is highly recommended, particularly two titles, Here and There in Astrology, and Simplified Horary Astrology, the former is available as a reprint on Amazon. The appeal in her work is how densely packed everything is, combined with A to Z’s expansive exposition, and you have a good overview of early 20th Century Astrology. Her table of essential dignities does a better job of mapping Neptune and Uranus than Llewelyn.

For a good overview of pre-Uranian astrology, William Lilly’s Christian Astrology is the classic text to read. I recommend Skyscript’s An Annotated Lilly, as the extensive footnotes of the first few chapters of Christian Astrology help explicate the one line to one word references to other figures of ancient, medieval, and renaissance astrology who influenced Lilly and the cultural milieu he lived in. Many of the referenced astrologers also have their works translated and available, so there’s a rabbit hole to chase down if you so choose.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content: I don't know if it's the best way, but there are some astrology correspondence courses available through renaissanceastrology.com. It costs some money, but it is nice to have a structured course, and there is someone to answer your questions. I'm partway through one of the courses, and am pretty happy with it so far.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content: Mine: I started out with the secret language of birthdays and Linda Goldman's Lovesigns. The first book is great to get a very basic run down of the signs, the elemental correspondences, planetary correspondences, what it means to be cardinal, fixed, or mutable, etc. And of course it goes through each day and gives a little run down a quote and other cool stuff. As far as calculating charts and giving deeper meanings or significance found in say, Qabalah, it won't teach you a thing. As for Linda Goodman, I wouldn't recommend her since she was one of those quacks popular in the 70's-80's (she lost me at fruitarian). Overall I don't know much to recommend, just where I started.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content:
Good day Steve,

There are many types of Astrology.
You got to know the differences between them
before you commit to one method.
The best way to do this is check internet sites
of knowledgeable people, read a bunch of articles
and get a feeling of their approaches.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content:
​Hi Steve,

John Frawley's The Horary Textbook is what I recommend for everyone and anyone interested in learning traditional astrology. Frawley starts at the bottom of the astrological hierarchy with horary (which he claims goes horary → electional → natal → mundane) and leads you through really everything you need to begin practicing. Like a number of people here, I'd struggled for some time to make sense of astrology, making very little progress with either contemporary (I think Parker's Astrology is trash) or near-contemporary books (A to Z Horoscope Maker is a dense and difficult introduction to the subject). After reading Horary Textbook I was casting horary charts and shocking myself with the accuracy of the predictions (though these were for simple things, "when will this package arrive", "when will I get my tax return", etc). Definitely check it out; also his website, though it no longer appears to be updated, has some good astrological essays as well. Hope this helps.

Best,
Andrew

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content: I too would love to hear any good book recommendations, but as a resource to just get your feet in the water, I recommend Chris Brennan's Astrology Podcast. I personally cannot stand just listening to people talk so podcasts are not my thing usually, but the monthly previews are useful, and recently Chris has been doing interviews with guest astrologers to go really in depth into each planet.  These podcasts are not short - 2-3 hours in many cases - but I tune in while I exercise and I learn something every time I listen. He's on YouTube and Spotify if those appeal to you.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content: Take a look at Richard Tarnas’ two book exploration of western philosophy.  It starts with The Passion of the Western Mind, a history of western thought from pre-history through the 20th century, without any particular focus on astrology.  The second book, Cosmos and Psyche, explores the connections between history and astrology.  One of the best volumes of its kind I’ve read.

replies.title: Re: Best way to get started with Astrology
replies.content:
If you want to spring for a course on natal astrology, I can recommend American Federation of Astrologers' SuperStar correspondence course (https://www.astrologers.com/learn-astrology/learn-astrology/). I'll soon begin the last lesson, and I now feel reasonably confident in my ability to cast and interpret a natal chart. But I think it's better to take the course after you've read some books first and they've whetted your interest enough that you are ready to pay more money to get a thorough overview and willing to take the time to do the coursework.

I'll toss out two more book possibilities. Amy Herring's book Essential Astrology: Everything You Need to Know to Interpret Your Natal Chart isn't up to the subtitle, but it'll get you familiar with the planets, signs, and houses. Alan Leo's The Key to Your Own Nativity has good descriptions of the way planets present in signs. Neither of these books treats aspects between planets well, or at all. The most valuable part of the course is that it teaches you how to understand the aspects and how they affect the natal chart. The course also teaches progressions and transits.

You can get your own natal chart for free here:

replies.content:
I want to thank everyone for their suggestions and advice. I really appreciate it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's certainly open to that. This and the books that follow are my attempt to make a publicly available equivalent of the sort of occult training you used to get from an occult school a century ago, by way of correspondence courses and the like. Magic as such has no role in it -- it's the kind of occult path that focuses on meditation and study, though the SoP is included as a basic ritual element and there are rituals of self-initiation that play an important role in the system. It's designed to be compatible with devotion to any deity (or none), and its goals are wisdom, revelation, and enlightenment.

I haven't yet decided whether to do a book specifically on spagyric alchemy in the system, but they're wholly compatible, and you could also plug in any other form of alchemy.

As for magic, that's not forbidden, it's just not part of the system. Students of the system who want to add magic can find workings that are compatible, with small modifications, in The Druid Magic Handbook and The Dolmen Arch (which is on its way to trade paperback reprint, btw).

No, I'm resolving the binary between magic and mysticism with a third option, which we can call occultism for want of a better label. Magic has to do with power, mysticism (at least in the Western tradition) has to do with love, and occultism has to do with wisdom.

In terms of the magical paths I've taught, their goal is personal transformation through participation in creative spiritual power.

comments.content:
What are some plants you would include in a garden to promote health of mind and spirit?

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content: Most herbs have an effect on mind and spirit and which ones you want in your garden depends on what effects you'd like. My book The Encyclopedia of Natural Magic covers a fair range of the options.

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content:
Hi,
I have a magic garden and recommend Saint John's wort, hyssop, roses, lilies, columbines, hollyhocks and sweet peas as a good beginner mix of magical plants along with the Angelica which is really nice. I have all these in my garden and it is a happy place.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content:
Maxine, that sounds wonderful.
Thank you for the suggestions.
Melodic Teal Hyena

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content:
Maxine's suggestions are all good, but I believe she lives in a mild, wet climate (BC, if I remember correctly Maxine?) Depending on where you live, your mileage with her recommendations may vary.

Gardening is very local, and the soil, climate, magical environment and the gardener all play a role in what will actually thrive. I would encourage you to commune with the plants, and notice what grows naturally; some "weeds" are powerfully protective magical herbs. Once you begin to welcome them and landscape with them in mind, good things may happen.

In turn, don't be discouraged if certain plants simply fail to grow for you. You can treat them as lessons; what about your soil, climate or gardening/magical self doesn't work for those plants? Some things you can change, some you can't, and some you won't want to; all this is powerful knowledge from the garden.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content:
Echoing this point, I have never been much of a gardener, but I got intrigued by the idea of native plants a ways back (I know our host has some things to say about a fixation on "native" plants and animals, but there can be some benefits as long as you're not puritanical about it). Among the advantages are that the plants are likely to be well-suited to the climate and not to need much more water than you'll get from rain once they're established. I live near the Gulf Coast of Texas, so I have a lot of prairie grasses and wildflowers, which have done very well. I don't know what kind of magical properties they may or may not have, but I definitely feel a much closer link to the spirits of the land than I used to.

You can find books and websites by searching for "[Region/State/City] native plants", and local organic nurseries are also full of helpful folks. The website Native American Seed (https://www.seedsource.com/) is excellent if you're looking for prairie grasses or wildflowers - both as a place to buy seeds and as a source for learning more.

On a more magico-religious note, I've also noticed a marked increase in growth after I started pouring out what is left from drink and food offerings to the gods in the garden, saying something like "For the land spirits, with ____'s leave", where _____ is the god(s)/goddess(es) the offering was first for.

replies.title: Re: Garden of Mind and Spirit
replies.content:
Hi there,

Without knowing anything about your climate, soil, etc, any plant suggestions you get might end in frustration… ;-)

Having said that, I‘d say go with what speaks to you - that would most likely be the plants which want to grow in your garden. Remember you don‘t have to stick with certain plants for the rest of your life if it doesn‘t work out!

I find that people who grow veggie gardens often think in terms of annuals, and people who have ornamental gardens often think in terms of perennials. What most people seem to overlook are the biannuals, though - and there are some very interesting plants in that category. Incidentally, a lot of them don‘t require good soil or „premium spots“.

Just tossing that out there on a hunch - maybe it will benefit you. ;-)

Enjoy the process!

Milkyway

comments.title: CGD Druid Grade Pathworkings
comments.content:
I wanted to double check I understand the ritual for the initial CGD Druid grade pathworkings to Muner.

We're working in the Druid temple with the circle and cross arranged circle above cross, so we open with:

Lesser Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram -> purify/consecrate/circumabulate -> invoke the sun -> Supreme Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram

But then the book says to perform the Calling of Earth. The Bardic grade pathworkings seemed to lump the elemental summoning ritual in with the Calling of Earth, but we've already summoned Earth in the supreme ritual of the pentagram, so do I perform the additional earth elemental ritual of the pentagram or just go straight on to "In the great name Cernunos..." in the north quarter of the ritual space?

In either case, from there I'll need to enter into meditation with color breathing and find myself in the inner grove of earth. Does "establishing myself in the inner grove of earth" include doing the earth elemental ritual of the pentagram in the inner grove of earth or do I just check around the grove for subconscious messages and proceed to call my guide? If I do the inner grove ritual of the pentagram, would I perform it again upon returning to the grove of earth from my pathworking to clean up? I seem to recall we don't banish in the inner groves.

replies.title: Re: CGD Druid Grade Pathworkings
replies.content:
1) You go straight on to "In the great name..." etc.

2) You do the Supreme Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram in the inner grove, and then proceed to call your guide. No, you don't do it again, and you don't banish in the inner grove, just in your own material plane grove (that is, the room where you're performing the ritual).

comments.title: Color Breathing and the Solar Plexus, Meditation Jerks, Merit of TV, Daily Dozen Update
comments.content:
Good evening,

Thanks as always for your generosity with your time and thoughtfulness. A few questions and a comment/update tonight:

1. A question on the visualization part of color breathing. For the past few months, I've been visualizing the nwyfre coming into my body through the skin in front the solar plexus, and my results have been a bit disappointing. It occurred to me this week that I might have been doing it wrong, and perhaps I should instead visualize the nwyfre coming in through the nose, down the throat, into the lungs, and then into the solar plexus from there. I've tried this second way and it seems like it might be working better. Is there a more common preferred way, or is this something where experimentation is the answer?

2. Related to the above: I recently had one of those "oh, I've been doing it wrong" experiences when I reviewed the earth gate in the DMH - I had not been visualizing the nwyfre of each gate entering my body through my solar plexus. Since I've started practicing it this way (about a week and a half, now), I've noticed a weaker feeling when I take on the blessings of each element. I have been doing the visualization more like the former above (coming in through the skin on the front of my body, rather than through the lungs). Might this be some kind of blockage? If so, anything to do about it besides keeping up the work?

3. A couple times now during meditation, I have found my muscles getting a bit "jerky" - it's not so dramatic as a "spasm", and it's not like my arms are flinging around, but if I don't tamp it down, my arms and legs move around more than a "twitch". Both times, it has seemed to be associated with thoughts/images of force rising up from the ground, and both times it has been when I've been somewhat tired. Is this something to be worried about, or just a normal growing pain? (If it helps, I've been doing daily discursive meditation with only a handful of missed days since the middle of may, and in the past I've done a variety of mindfulness/breathing focused meditations daily for up to several months, and sitting still is usually not a problem)

4. This may not be precisely a magical question, but after listening to you mention TV on a couple of podcasts, I'm curious: do you believe that the medium of "moving pictures on a screen" is inherently not very worthwhile and/or its potential is played out, or is it more that the cacomagical system that produces pretty much all TV and film results in a bunch of dreck in a medium that might otherwise have potential? For example, if a gifted individual made a film entirely on their own, with no outside control or funding, might that have some artistic/spiritual merit? Or is it that moving, photo-realistic images is inherently bad for the imagination?

5. Not a question, but an occult-adjacent update for anyone interested in old-timey fitness: I've been experimenting with using Indian clubs during the "Daily Dozen" workouts that JMG discovered were ancestral to the workouts he was taught in the uniformed wing of an initiatory order. They turn out to pair very well! I would recommend 1 lb clubs for daily/near daily flexibility and conditioning, though I've also tried out 3 lb clubs, which gives one hell of a workout, especially to your grip and forearms, so likely very useful for anyone working on swordsmanship. (BTW, if you read the last sentence and thought to yourself "3 lbs, are you kidding?", that was my initial reaction - the fact that the weight is off-center from your grip makes a huge difference. I'm no weakling - I can do a handful of pull-ups on gymnastic rings and deadlift 285 lbs ~3 times, and I find it a challenge to complete the Daily Dozen with a 3 lb weight without resting.) If you don't have Indian clubs, I imagine you could get similar benefits from dumbbells with slightly more weight - maybe ~3 lb for minimal extra work and ~5 lb for a real challenge. I'm also working on a few supplements. There's not much emphasis on the legs, which I suspect is an artifact of the exercises being originally developed for the army (where you would be running your tokas off everyday anyway), so you might consider combining with running/biking/squats/deadlifts/leg-workout-of-your-choice for working out your whole body. I'm also experimenting with pairing with some other Indian club movements that have more rotation of the wrists and elbows, as I do a lot of typing and want to keep my hands/wrists healthy. Between (nearly) Daily Dozen working out and (nearly) thrice daily Sun Salutations, I've seen a marked improvement in flexibility, even just over the past few weeks. No updates on the spiritual/energetic side of these workouts just yet, but I'll certainly share if/when I observe any. If you're interested in trying the Daily Dozen, I found the video instructions on this channel the most helpful for learning them: https://www.youtube.com/c/Internalforcefitness/videos

Thanks very much,
Jeff

In the KJV, "Thou shalt have no other gods before me." Emphasis mine.

As a counterpoint, later on, again KJV, Exodus 22: "Thou shalt not revile the gods..."

Later on, St. Paul would say that (paraphrasing), sure, there are other gods out there, much like there are other rulers, but we (Christians) regard them as evil demons.

I'm no professional theologian, so this is just my syncretistic, personal, effectively pagan take: In the first quote, YHVH is speaking directly to Moses and the Jews about the covenant. As a gentile, this covenant does not necessarily hold in the same way; I honor the Triune god along with the other gods, and suspect he may be the demiurge (although I think it can and has been argued that all gods may be able to fulfill the demiurgic function--that level of theorizing, though, is beyond my limited wisdom and open for debate.)

My personal take, again, is that different gods, as supreme individuals, have different inclinations. If we regard YHVH as the demiurge (NOTE: Not "The One," but the demiurge), then arguably other pantheons are responsible for different facets of nature and human nature. Jesus, if we regard him as the Logos, seems to have a particular interest in compassion and redemption, whereas Zeus/Jupiter has a particular interest in justice, for example. The divine light is a spectrum.

And as I believe Roberto Calasso put it: There is nothing sadder than a sacrifice to the wrong god.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

I'm a Catholic, so in my theology there are different degrees of worship/veneration: latria, hyperdulia, and dulia.  Latria is the worship reserved for the Holy Trinity alone.  This is done chiefly in the Holy Sacrifice of the Mass.  Dulia is the honor or veneration given to the saints, and hyperdulia is the special devotion and veneration given to the Virgin Mary.  I offer dulia to gods and spirits that I do not think are in conflict with the values and commandments of the Holy Trinity.  The differences between these three types of worship are largely an internal matter: "[Jesus] said to him, 'You shall love the Lord, your God, with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind. This is the greatest and the first commandment'" (Matt. 22:37-38).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Obviously not JMG but;

The first thing to remember is that the Hermetica was written over a period of several centuries by a number of different authors. They don't all have the same view on things, and despite having enough common ground to be identifiable part of the same tradition, some of them go off in some rather odd directions.

The scholastic method is to assume that apparent contradiction between authoritative sources is the result of a personal error. If something seems off, the first thing to do is examine the definitions of terms being used. Idiosyncratic definitions can result in some really odd claims unfolding from perfectly sound reasoning. Barring that, the second question is what sort of observations or insights would lead to this conclusion. It is not enough to say that somebody is wrong, if you want to claim to understand them, it is also necessary to be able to explain what insights would be misused to result in their error.

It has been a long time since I read the Hermetica. I believe good is usually understood in a platonic sense in a hermetic context. Evil is somewhat trickier, both Cacos and Harma are both frequently redered as evil. Cacos has really strong connotations of insanity, while Harma is traditionally associated with incompetence (literally missing the mark). The contrast between good and evil, to use less loaded language, is the contrast between health and illness or wisdom and idiocy. If the human capacity to reason is predicated on an external power (to eu, or the platonic good) then it is quite natural to see humans working on their own as dumb and crazy.

1. In former days, in far-off years when... The heavens were grieved and the earth groaned at evening time, the gods... To mankind, they became appeased and granted them abundance

2. To guide the land and establish the peoples they appointed a king. ... To rule the black-headed, the many peoples,

3. The king planted the palm in his courtyard... He planted the tamarisk. In the shade of the tamarisk he arranged a banquet

4. A banquet; in the shade of the palm....

5-10 The Tamarisk opened his mouth and spoke. He addressed the Date Palm: "My body ...... the bodies of the gods. (The reference is to statues of tamarisk wood.) You grow your fruits but someone places them before me like a maid approaching her mistress. You do not provide the measuring vessels. You are ...... minor crops, but I ....... Your attendants ...... before me for you."

11-19 In his anger the Date Palm answered him. He addressed his brother the Tamarisk: "You say: "If people build daises for me and beautify them too, they certainly do not swear by the gods before clay (?)." -- You may be the body of the gods in their shrines and people may name with a good name the daises of the gods, but it is silver that can pride itself as the overlay of the gods. ......, describe your beauty!"

replies.title: Re: Time manipulation
replies.content:
It's not especially weird at all. The so-called "Mandela Effect" reveals something very important, but it doesn't have anything to do with timelines.

There were two leading figures in the anti-apartheid movement in South Africa in the 1970s, Nelson Mandela and Stephen Biko. Biko was murdered by the South African police in 1977, and his funeral was a huge event, with 200,000 people in attendance and plenty of play in world media. Fast forward to 2000 or so, when everyone remembered Nelson Mandela and most people outside of Africa had forgotten about Biko, and that memory got blurred and reformulated.

This happens because, as psychologists and occultists alike know, memories aren't like videotapes of what happened. You reconstruct and reformulate your memories every single time you recall them, and they drift, blur, and change over time -- this has been shown repeatedly in experiments. That's why so many people who grew up reading the Berenstain Bears muddle the name up with the composer Leonard Bernstein, Frankenstein's monster, and all the other examples of the much more common -stein ending, and remember it spelled "Berenstein." It's also why if ten people witness the same car crash, no two of them describe it the same way. Memory is much closer to imagination than most people realize -- and it's pretty much only occultists who realize that you can use this effect to redefine your own past and change its impact on your present life.

I've never encountered a magical system that used algebra, much less anything more complex than that. comments.title: Magical hygiene for a new home
comments.content:
Hi John,

Sorry if you’ve answered this question previously. I’ve just bought a fixer upper house and I want to clean out any spiritual gunk that may be lingering. I don’t practice magic but I do lurk here and follow some of the conversations.

If I’m not a practitioner, are there still things I can do to clean the place besides the SOP and related rituals? I think you had mentioned a Hoodoo floor wash at one point in the past. Anything else?

I don’t notice any bad vibes in the place but I figure good “spiritual hygiene” is always prudent.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Magical hygiene for a new home
replies.content: Sure. Into a bucket of hot water, put a cup of white vinegar, a quarter cup of salt, and a tablespoon or so of hot pepper, the hottest you can find. Use that to mop all the floors of the house, starting in the kitchen and ending at the front door. That's a classic hoodoo floor wash and will clean the place up nicely.

replies.title: Re: Magical hygiene for a new home
replies.content: I seem to recall a similar Hoodoo mix for carpeted floors, something like a carpet powder that one vacuums up and disposes of.  Is that right?  Most of my house has hardwood floors but the master bedroom has carpet.

replies.title: Re: Magical hygiene for a new home
replies.content: Kosher salt and two kinds of hot pepper. (In hoodoo odd numbers of ingredients are powerful.) Sprinkle it all over the carpet, leave it for a few hours (overnight if you can), then vacuum it up and get rid of the contents of the vacuum at once, outdoors. (An outdoor garbage can is fine.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

In an irregular way I've taken up the practice of discursive meditation based on the instructions you posted here a couple years ago. But I haven't gone very far with it, all things considered, and I've come to realize it's because I don't fully believe I'm "doing it right." So I figure I should just ask how this strikes you.

The main sticking point is that I haven't, at least as of yet, "signed on" with any one system of spirituality. I'm chary of mixing and matching elements of spiritual traditions without having developed any competence for such things, so aside from a few meditations in one of the traditions I've felt some call toward (which, however, doesn't have much of a formal textual tradition that I know of), I've ended up meditating on questions to do with how to live my life—using discursive meditation for secular purposes, you could say.

It does seem to work great as a way of clearing away distractions and focusing on some question that's been worrying or nagging at me so I can get it into the light and work through it. However, you mention meditating on a dense, pregnant phrase from a traditional text, and generally with this I'm meditating on pretty prosaic questions, like "What kind of person is such-and-such character from the novel I'm planning?" They're also usually questions rather than statements, which means that I find myself searching for an answer rather than just looking for layers of meaning, though I do use this only for open-ended questions that seem to permit of some depth.

Do you think any of this risks getting me into bad habits for when, someday, I do find a particular body of lore to work with intensively? And is there anything else you'd say about the advisability or inadvisability of using discursive meditation for secular questions?

Nathanael
Monochrome Vacuous Owl

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Discursive meditation is very flexible, and yes, it can be used for writing purposes, and for other practical ends. I tend to talk about its application to Big Questions simply because that's what most beginning occultists like to explore. If you're at all concerned, just make time once a week or so to tackle a Big Question that interests you. That'll stretch your meditating mind and keep it nimble enough to handle whatever you end up studying.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Many thanks for this! I'll proceed with a clear mind and conscience—seems the best way to proceed.

comments.title: Gratitude
comments.content:
Wanted to say thank you to JMG and commentariat for many things in the past few years that helped me find my way to a Catholic Latin Mass yesterday.

I had an odd upbringing in that when I was a young child my father was a lay leader with the Society of St. Pius X, which was a Catholic dissident group that continued to ordain priests and hold Latin masses after Vatican II. When the leaders were excommunicated in the late 1980s, the congregation we had been attending fell apart and then I spent a couple of years attending Episcopalian services with my mother, then a brief time attending English-language Catholic services, then about a decade with the Unitarian-Universalists, then about a decade of keeping my distance from organized religions and some tentative exploration of Druidry. Another complicating factor keeping me away from the Catholics: the pedophile priest scandal and the coverups and gaslighting of victims.

In the last few months, seeing a few comments here about the Latin Mass as a powerful white magic ritual, at a time when the demons and evil are so prevalent, and then a couple of other synchronicities, I looked around online and learned that since 2007, with recognition from Pope Benedict, the Latin Mass is being done in more places, more regularly, and one of them is close to where I live.

Being there yesterday was strange for lots of reasons, but powerful and I think I will continue attending and trying to learn more.

Thank you to JMG and everyone here.

replies.title: Re: Gratitude
replies.content: You're most welcome. I hope it works well for you.

replies.title: Re: Gratitude
replies.content: I can remember the first time I received sacraments in the traditional rites of the Church: it was like the difference between night and day, hot and cold.  The effects of the traditional rites were so profound, I think it was a genuine TSW moment for me.  I'm glad to hear that you've found a TLM near you.  It is a great treasure that was buried in the aftermath of Vatican II, and the losses are incalculable.

comments.title: Infernal Infiltration
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks for providing this forum. Demonic influence has been a topic of much discussion lately and I have a couple related questions.

1. The authors of the Goetia were of the opinion that a faithful Christian could bind demons to their service, that as fallen angels they were evil when free but could serve good ends when properly corralled and commanded. Were they wrong? Overly optimistic? Is it possible to do as they describe but too challenging for any but the most expert magicians to attempt? My inclination is to never have any truck with such entities, but I'm wondering what the medieval summoners were thinking.

2. I'm interested in learning how demonolatry spread within neopagan and leftist communities. The most concise history I've found of it is your Flight to the Fringes essay (link for any who haven't seen it):

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-flight-to-the-fringes-and-what-waits-there/

Has anyone from inside the neopagan scene written about this? You've mentioned that some were critical of dealing with evil forces but were shouted down by others. It would be interesting to get a view from inside the movement of what happened there and the effects it's had on practitioners.

To digress for a bit, for the left and neopagans to embrace the demonic seems like a natural part of the rejection of "respectability politics" that they've been harping on for the last decade, along with the growing sentiment that "there are no bad tactics, just bad targets." Even to someone who rejects any belief in the spiritual, this should be taken as a sign the aforementioned movements feel that their desired ends justify any means.

If you know of any other histories that have been written of this infernal infiltration I'd appreciate knowing about them.

I appreciate your time.

replies.title: Re: Infernal Infiltration
replies.content:
1) I consider that claim from the Goetia to be appallingly misguided. A genuine mystic who experiences direct contact with the Divine can command demons safely. Anyone else? Not a chance. Demons are smarter than we are, they're extremely good at manipulating us, and they're motivated by malice and hatred. What's more, anything you think you can get by dealing with demons, you can really achieve through your own powers -- you just have to work for it. The temptation of demonolatry is the lure of something for nothing, the delusion that someone else will do the work for you. Fall for it, and whatever you think you've achieved by it will turn on you in the end.

2) I left the Neopagan scene and shook the dust of it from my shoes once I stepped down from the Grand Archdruid's position in AODA in 2015. I haven't been back, and have no idea whether there's been anything written on the subject from inside the community. If you can find something, I'd like to hear about it.

comments.title: Decolonization of Astrology
comments.content:
Alice Sparkly Kat (actual name) has a book out making the rounds about decolonizing astrology.

http://www.alicesparklykat.com/articles/114/Decolonizing_Essential_Dignities/ From her site:
I’d like to start this article by saying how much I hate it when white astrologers talk about planets in detriment (which is also called planets in exile) by comparing them to people who are vacationing in some foreign country. Apparently, things are hard when you have to read a map to get around or when you don’t know how to order food. Apparently, that’s how planets in detriment behave—like they’re tourists who are visiting some foreign land.

To understand essential dignities, you have to understand citizenship. Within Hellenistic astrology, which is also the type of astrology that the Romans used, citizenship was incorporated into cosmology. As Sylvia Wynter writes, “Greek astronomy was to remain an ethno-astronomy. One, that is, in which the moral/political laws of the Greek polis had been projected upon the physical cosmos, enabling them to serve as “objective truth” in Feyerabend’s (1987) sense of the term, and therefore as, in my own terms, adaptive truth-for the Greeks.”

Rome was all about citizenship. If you were not a citizen in Rome, then you were a slave. The Roman economy ran on the mining of precious metals but only slaves could be used as miners. If you were born inside of Rome, even if you are the child of a slave, then you were a citizen. Thus, Rome had to constantly expand so that they could conquer new people to enslave. They had to keep mining. To be a citizen within Rome was to be represented within the state.

The United States of America was designed by people who liked to cosplay as Romans. American ideas around citizenship are an example of neoclassicism—a new interpretation of Rome within modernity. Citizenship within the United States has always been based on race, even more so than within Europe (when the French Revolution happened, most of the people living in France did not identify as French).

Race in America is all about who is a settler and who is a transient. Transients are criminalized while settlers are protected. Indigenous Americans were considered transient, as were Africans, as were Chinese coolies, as were Mexicans. Only White people were considered settlers, as being at home.

....
Within the essential dignity scheme within astrology, there are dignified planets, debilitated planets, exalted planets, and fallen planets. For this article, we’re only going to be talking about domicile rulership and not the exaltations or falls, since domicile lordship is where this citizenship business comes in the strongest. When a planet is in its own sign, it is its own domicile lord. That place is said to be at home, in its own place of belonging, and comfortable. When a planet is in detriment or is debilitated, it is said to be in exile or away from its rulership. It is ruled by others. In a Hegelian framework, planets that are dignified are masters and planets that are debilitated are slaves.

So, debilitated planets are not like some person who is simply vacationing in a place where they don’t know the language or whatever. It fucking sucks to compare exile to tourism. Tourism is an extremely privileged way to encounter the world. When you are a tourist, you are encountering a place that was already recreated for you. When you are a tourist, you are extending your own comfort.

Debilitation is a state of existence in which you are always viewed to be an outsider, where you must constantly prove your own existence, in places that were made to exclude you.

The astrologer Doelow da Pilotman from Flight Astrology on Youtube has a good way of thinking about rulerships that I use all the time. He compares planets that are in detriment to planets that are at work, saying that you can’t really relax when you’re at work. You always have a boss that’s breathing down your neck. Planets in rulership are like planets that are at home. You can do whatever you want when you’re at home but, sometimes, because you can do whatever you want, you don’t really do anything.

This is a useful way of thinking about essential dignities because, when you’re at work, you’re in a white world. Industry is centered around whiteness as power. When you leave your house and behave as an economic agent, then you’re not going to be comfortable because you’re in a position of serving power. When you’re at home, you’re not participating in white economies. You’re in your own space.
.....
When we look at a chart using the scheme of essential rulerships, we’re looking at a cosmology that was made within the ethno-cosmology of the Romans. Within modern astrology, this ethno-cosmology has been recreated as modern race relations. When we look at a chart using the scheme of essential rulerships, we’re looking at how modern power dynamics may affect your comfort levels and your struggles. We’re not looking at some planets that are chilling at home and some planets that are traveling around.
---------------------

Um, what is she talking about? Is she mixing up unrelated things in a giant salad blender and calling it post-colonial astrology?

Have you any opinion on this idea of astrology (i.e. Western Tropical) being colonial? It seems to be all the rage in certain quarters these days.

Perplexed Purple Possum

Ms. Sparkly Kat has found something she can be offended about, which is of course de rigueur if you want status in today's privileged classes, and so she's come up with a way of denouncing all of astrology as "colonialist" in order to parade her superior virtue and sensitivity. comments.title: Memory magic
comments.content:
It occurs to me that one basic form of memory magic might be:

1) Imagine some event that didn't happen but, if it had, would have had a lasting and positive impact on you.

2) Imagine who you would be today if that had happened.

It seems to me that the detail put into (1) can be sparse and impressionistic -- an image or three, a snippet of a conversation, and most importantly a feeling; though smell would probably help a lot -- but (2) needs to be imagined as full and palpably as possible, since it's the "What you contemplate, you imitate" step.

The specification that it's an event that didn't happen is important: adding to your stock of memories strikes me as much easier than trying to make some event disappear from your history, and a single, powerful memory is better than an attempt to relive your life from scratch (I've actually tried both of these misguided techniques in the past -- they don't work). What you can do, of course, is imagine an event that has a compensatory effect on you for something negative that happened to you.

It would probably be useful to pair this with some natural magic -- incense, for example -- or some light ritual, though that's going beyond my pay grade at this point.

Any suggestions? Any risks to this technique that I'm not seeing? It does occur to me that pretending something happened that didn't has some of the same risks as New Thought affirmations in terms of sliding into self-delusion.

(As always, thank you for hosting this and fielding our questions!)

replies.title: Re: Memory magic
replies.content: This is certainly an option. I don't think (1) should be light and impressionistic, though -- make it as vivid and exact as possible, and then do the same thing with (2), and you'll get better effects. As for the risks, as long as you remember that it's an affirmation and not a reality, you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Memory magic
replies.content:
Here's one take on your proposal, excerpted from a blog post on my dreamwidth account: Deena Metzger, in her Writing for Your Life, recounts a story of a woman with whom she worked who had suffered greatly as a child and who had a tremendous sense of lacking that she couldn't repair. Metzger suggested she "rewrite" her early-life story to include a helpful aunt who'd existed in real life but who hadn't been overtly present. The writer wrote her new story and remade her history in a way that allowed for her suffering but also included someone who had cared and helped in many ways. She imagined scenes, in great detail, where the aunt had been present, and adopted them as her story - not in a deluded sense, but in a sort of "double-vision" way that allowed for the imaginal realm to grant a healing possibility that hadn't existed in the "real" realm...

...Metzger went on to write about the woman who "created" her history: "I don't think L. M. ever forgot that she had created this aunt, but she did allow herself to ignore this fact. Her aunt thrived in her imagination and supported her life. Over a period of time, L. M.'s life changed. She dared to do things that she couldn't have dared before. She became energetic, motivated. She saw herself as capable. The last time we met, she showed me a portfolio she was taking to the movie studios...'I never could have done this without my aunt," she said with a wink.'"

replies.title: Re: Memory magic
replies.content: That's an awesome story. Thank you!
